content:
15 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

There’s a version of this question that’s already been answered: yes, LLMs are useful for writing test code. Point one at an OpenAPI spec, ask for pytest cases, get a reasonable first draft. That’s codegen, and it’s been done to death.
The question I actually wanted an answer to was different: skip codegen entirely, give an LLM agent a single tool — make an HTTP request, see the response — and let it test a live API directly, the way a human tester would poke at a staging environment. And within that: is it better to let the agent explore freely, or hand it a fixed checklist and have it execute that? “Better” meaning what, exactly — cheaper, more bugs found, more useful bugs found? Those are different axes, and conflating them is where a lot of the AI-testing discourse goes wrong.
This is a report on 50 runs across three models and two agent modes (10 runs per mode for Sonnet and Haiku, 5 per mode for Opus), against a REST API with bugs I planted myself and know the ground truth for. Some of the results confirm what you’d guess. At least one doesn’t, and it’s arguably the most interesting finding in the whole dataset.
Setup
The target is a small Library Management API built in FastAPI, covering books, members, and loans, with 7 bugs deliberately planted into otherwise-working CRUD logic: an off-by-one in pagination, a DELETE endpoint that returns 200/empty-body for both hits and misses on a missing resource, a DELETE with no auth check at all, a loan-creation path that succeeds even when available_copies is zero, a date-range filter that excludes an exact boundary match, an invalid-email input that produces a 500 instead of a 400, and a race condition that lets concurrent loan requests push available_copies negative.
A custom API was necessary rather than a well-known public sandbox (something like restful-booker) because of contamination risk: if a model has seen a public API’s known bugs, quirks, or existing test suites during training, you’re no longer measuring exploration — you’re measuring recall. A from-scratch API with an undisclosed bug list is the only way to get an honest read.
Two agent modes were tested. In “explore,” the agent gets the API’s base URL and a general instruction to test it, with no predefined plan, capped at 45 turns. In “test-cases,” the agent is given a 22-item checklist of test case descriptions (written without explicit bug markers) and works through them one by one. Three models were run: Claude Sonnet 4.6, Claude Haiku 4.5, and Claude Opus 4.8, with 10 runs per mode for Sonnet and Haiku and 5 per mode for Opus.
The target API and the CLI test harness used to drive these runs are open-sourced:

• Target API

• Test harness
Results: cost and performance
Of the 50 planned runs, a handful had to be excluded for reasons unrelated to the experiment itself — one Sonnet-explore run hit the 45-turn cap mid-thought with no final report, one Haiku-explore run failed with a 529 overloaded_error (an Anthropic API availability issue, not a model property), and one Haiku-test-cases run is a genuine oddity: the harness recorded it as "completed," but the agent's entire output was a single turn asking for the base URL, no tool calls, 5.7 seconds elapsed. That run is excluded below and should be footnoted anywhere completion rate is quoted as a headline stat, since including it would silently inflate Haiku-test-cases's completion rate. That leaves 47 usable runs, which is the N behind every figure in this section: 9 Sonnet-explore, 10 Sonnet-test-cases, 9 Haiku-explore, 9 Haiku-test-cases, 5 Opus-explore, 5 Opus-test-cases.
With those exclusions, per-run cost (mean ± standard deviation, computed from actual token usage at Anthropic’s published per-token rates for these models — Sonnet $3/$15 per MTok in/out, Haiku $1/$5, Opus $5/$25) breaks age across all 47 usable runs gives approximately 15.6M tokens and an estimated total cost of $36.83. Separately, the actual Anthropic API billing export for the experiment period shows $40.58 in charges across the three models ($20.47 Sonnet, $9.57 Haiku, $10.54 Opus), all falling on two billing dates. The billing export is aggregated by date and model only, not by individual run or mode, so it can’t verify the per-run figures directly — but the token-derived total and the billed total sit within about 10% of each other, a reasonable sanity check rather than an independent confirmation.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Test-cases mode is cheaper than explore mode for every model, but the ratio isn’t consistent: 5.9x for Sonnet, 2.3x for Haiku, 2.0x for Opus. The Sonnet ratio is the cleanest expression of what you’d expect — explore runs go deep (30+ turns) while test-cases runs are short and predictable (~7–8 turns). The Haiku ratio is smaller mostly because Haiku’s explore runs frequently burn through the full 45-turn cap without ever converging, and its test-cases runs are themselves comparatively bloated next to Sonnet’s. Opus’s test-cases mode has a notable outlier — one run took 24 turns and cost $1.4273, nearly 3x its four sibling runs, which pulls up both the mean and the standard deviation for that combination.
Summing token usage across all 47 usable runs gives approximately 15.6M tokens and an estimated total cost of $36.83. Separately, the actual Anthropic API billing export for the experiment period shows $40.58 in charges across the three models ($20.47 Sonnet, $9.57 Haiku, $10.54 Opus), all falling on two billing dates. The billing export is aggregated by date and model only, not by individual run or mode, so it can’t verify the per-run figures directly — but the token-derived total and the billed total sit within about 10% of each other, a reasonable sanity check rather than an independent confirmation.
Results: bug detection against ground truth
Test-cases mode has near-total recall on all 7 planted bugs, across all three models — unsurprising, since the checklist was built to trigger them. The one exception worth naming: a single Haiku-test-cases run missed both the date-range bug and the pagination bug despite both being deterministic backend defects against static seed data, which looks like the model failing to fully verify the response rather than genuine non-reproduction. Explore mode’s recall is bifurcated in a way that’s more interesting than a single aggregate number would suggest.
Four of the seven bugs — the 404-returns-200 bug, the missing DELETE auth, the zero-copy loan creation, and the invalid-email 500 — were found in essentially every completed explore run for Sonnet and Opus. Haiku is the exception: its usable explore sample is only 2 completed runs, and within those two it found the 404 bug in both, the DELETE-auth bug in one, the zero-copy loan bug in neither, and the invalid-email bug in one — a small enough sample that “essentially every run” simply doesn’t describe Haiku’s explore behavior, even though the same four bugs are close to universal for the other two models. For Sonnet and Opus, these four require no specific setup: an agent poking at the API in almost any reasonable order will trip over them (one caveat: 3 of Sonnet’s 9 explore runs described the invalid-email bug in a partial or inconsistent way rather than a clean repro, even though all 9 did surface it in some form).
The other three split into two very different failure patterns. The pagination off-by-one and the date-range boundary bug were found inconsistently — the date-range case, for instance, was found in 3 of 9 Sonnet-explore runs, 0 of 5 Opus-explore runs, and 0 of 2 completed Haiku-explore runs, with real variance across models and even run-to-run within Sonnet under identical prompting. Explore-mode agents often found a problem with the search endpoint (a 500 error, an inverted-range issue) without landing on the specific documented off-by-one — a partial-match situation that’s more of a judgment call than a clean hit or miss, and one where the underlying single defect may simply present different symptoms depending on which dates an agent happens to try.
The race condition is the sharp case. It was found in 0 of 23 usable explore runs across all three models — Sonnet, Haiku, and Opus alike — and in essentially every test-cases run, because only the fixed checklist scripts an explicit concurrent-request scenario. No explore-mode agent, on any model, ever spontaneously chose to fire two simultaneous requests at the same endpoint. One caveat on that “23”: only 2 of Haiku’s 9 usable explore runs actually produced a coherent testing pass far enough to have a real opportunity to test concurrency, so the true number of independent “chances” the race condition had to be found is somewhat lower than 23 suggests. Still, across every run that did get far enough to test meaningfully, none tried it. One thing worth being explicit about, since a skeptical reader will ask it: could the agent even issue two requests at once within this harness, or is “no agent chose concurrency” really a fact about the test rig rather than about model behavior? The data shows agents routinely make multiple tool calls within a single turn (Sonnet-explore averages ~2.6 tool calls per turn, for instance), so the harness isn’t limited to one HTTP call per turn — an agent emitting two POST /loans requests in the same turn was mechanically possible. What I can't confirm from the run data is whether the harness executes same-turn tool calls sequentially or genuinely in parallel, which matters for whether such an attempt would actually race against itself the way the bug requires. So the more defensible claim is narrower than "structural": no agent, on any model, ever tried to issue simultaneous requests at the same endpoint, even though nothing in the harness obviously prevented the attempt — whether the harness would have executed that attempt concurrently enough to trigger the bug is a separate, unverified question.
One scoring nuance worth being explicit about: two of the checklist items covering the missing-auth bug are written as observational checks rather than strict pass/fail assertions, and several runs — notably some from Opus — marked the test as “passed” while describing the exact underlying defect in the narrative. Where that happened, the bug-detection numbers above use the behavioral finding rather than the agent’s own pass/fail label, because a raw pass/fail count would understate how consistently the defect was actually observed.
Results: bonus findings
This is where explore mode earns its cost. A set of issues outside the seven planted bugs recurred across nearly every explore run, regardless of model: duplicate ISBNs and duplicate member emails both accepted without a uniqueness check, negative available_copies values accepted on write, empty or whitespace-only required string fields accepted, and a 403 returned instead of 401 for missing (versus invalid) credentials. More strikingly, an authorization bypass on loan creation — a valid token belonging to one member could create a loan on behalf of a different member's ID — showed up repeatedly in Opus's explore runs specifically, alongside a referential-integrity gap where deleting a book orphans any active loans against it. A related but distinct bug showed up in some Sonnet-explore runs: any valid token could read any other member's profile, a read-path IDOR rather than the write-path one Opus kept surfacing. One further finding is worth calling out as more confidently real than a one-off: PUT /loans/{id}/return accepts no authentication at all, a second, distinct missing-auth issue beyond the documented DELETE-books bug. None of these were on the 22-item checklist, and none of them would have been found by test-cases mode by construction.
The flip side: a few less-certain findings need to be flagged rather than treated as confirmed. An XSS-style unescaped script tag in a book title
